content.problemStatement:
Aman and Akshat are trying to solve a task given to them by their teacher. They are given a pile of stones containing NNN stones with an integer written on each of them. There are two different kinds of moves that can be performed on the pile : 



Player will remove a stone from the toptoptop of the pile and put it back on the bottombottombottom of the pile 

Player will remove a stone from the toptoptop of the pile and throwthrowthrow it away. 

Aman in his turn will perform move 111 once and then move 222 once. Akshat in his turn will perform move 111 twice and then move 222 once.

They will stop making moves when there is only 111 stone left in the pile.

Both of them gets turn alternatively with Aman going first. Find the person performing the last move and the number written on the last stone left in the pile.

The stones at index i′thi'thi′th is located higher than the stones at index j′thj'thj′th , such that (i<j)(i \lt j)(i<j)


Input Format

The first line contains an integer TTT, representing TTT Testcases.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN, representing the size of Array AAA.

The second line of each test case contains NNN integer, representing array AAA



Output Format

For each test case, Print 222 space-separated integer representing the person making the last move (111 for Aman and 000 for Akshat) and the number written on the stone remaining.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^6
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases.
For each test case:
- The first line contains an integer N (the number of stones).
- The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN, the numbers written on the stones from top to bottom.
content.outputFormat:
For each test case output a single line with two space‑separated values:
- 1 if Aman performed the last move, otherwise 0 (Akshat).
- The integer written on the stone that remains after all moves.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case (N=3, odd) ends with Akshat (0) and stone -5. Second (N=4, even) ends with Aman (1) and stone 2. Third (N=6, even) ends with Aman (1) and stone 0.
Only one turn (Aman's). After rotating 10 to bottom we have [20,10]; discarding 20 leaves 10. N is even, so Aman performed the last move.

solution.approach: The pile can be treated as a circular queue. A move of type 1 (rotate) is equivalent to removing the front element and appending it to the back. A move of type 2 (discard) simply removes the front element. Aman’s turn consists of one rotation followed by one discard, Akshat’s turn consists of two rotations followed by one discard. Each turn discards exactly one stone, therefore after N‑1 turns only one stone stays. The player of the last turn is known from the parity of N: if N is even the last turn is Aman (output 1), otherwise it is Akshat (output 0). The remaining stone can be obtained by simulating the process with a deque, performing O(1) operations per turn, giving O(N) time per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(ΣN) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the deque storing the current pile.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys
from collections import deque

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        dq = deque()
        for _ in range(N):
            dq.append(int(next(it)))
        aman = True
        while len(dq) > 1:
            rot = 1 if aman else 2
            for _ in range(rot):
                dq.append(dq.popleft())
            dq.popleft()  # discard
            aman = not aman
        last_mover = 1 if N % 2 == 0 else 0
        out_lines.append(f"{last_mover} {dq[0]}")
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
